item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was a bit disorienting to have "what is a man!?" next to a setup that I would normally associate with Dr. Wondertainment's Littlest Best Buddy-Pal-Friend and/or Prometheus Labs Fuck-Up #38,952, but I think that this manages to work overall. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gladly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This skip reminds me of GIR from Invader Zim but more competent. I want one. Take my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: IIRC "life to a friend" would be "Vita ad amicum" - "friend" is the object of the preposition, so it should be accusative, and "life" is nominative.

item.title: Re: I really love this
item.content:encoded:
Glad you enjoyed the article.

"Dungeon Master" and "Johnny" were both fun to write, though the philosophy angle is in large part due to the help I received from A Random Day during the drafting phrase.

item.title: I really love this
item.content:encoded:
I love how 3960-3 was very philosophical, as was SCP-3960-4.
Good job. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed.

I'm going to say you're probably a more effective translator than google, and I appreciate your feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of the SCP, the only thing I noticed which you may want to look into is the Latin 'Vita est amicus'. I am fairly sure that 'est' is Latin for is, whilst the Latin for to or towards is 'ad'. Furthermore, Vita should be replaceable with Vitam which is the accusative singular of vita, so the sentence would be 'Vitam Amicus'.

Now, my knowledge of Latin only went as far as secondary school, so if anyone can help more please do, and please tell me where I'm wrong so I can correct myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh hey, that is a Meeseeks line isn't it.

Coincidental, I assure you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did Gizmo paraphrase a Meeseeks?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
3960-3: She responded with "What kind of love do you get when you create something for just to love you?

This feels grammatically confusing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Save the docs, the completed units, and the rest of the unassembled prototypes. Vince says we might have a buyer at another company for everything as is. Heard the name "Isabelle" a couple times. Isaac is still writing up the details. I'll let you know what the HPAs' fate will be as soon as I do.

Why take someone down when you can buy them out…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Makes you wonder why they haven't obliterated GAW yet…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was neat. Good thing for Anderson these didn't make it to market, or Wondertainment would come down on them like a squeaky hammer of competitive justice!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll take it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The whole sentient doll thing, with or without religious overtones, has been done quite a bit, and the emails dragged some. (Edit: Specifically thinking of SCP-237)

Still, it's well written, and that last log turned something I was initially a little annoyed with (the religious comment) right on its head. Plus good Foundation development. So +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I felt like the first two interview logs didn't add much to the article. Things didn't really start picking up until the third one, which is easily the best imo. Enjoyed the Wondertainment connection, even if I don't really see them outsourcing R&D on new products to outfits like Anderson or Prometheus. Nevertheless I found it to be enjoyable in the end and appreciated the exploration of some deeper concepts through friendly robots. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the core idea, the development of the product, the insight into the process via email chain (fitting for the Anderson Robotics setting, and I feel as if I've seen similar flavor in Anderson tales before), and the logs give it another level of content that puts more focus on what sapient toys might think about in their spare time.

+ Nitpick enclosed
- Damn you're too nitpicky

I do think that choosing to have DM overhear the theological conversation in log 3 seems a little heavy handed, however. Johnny seems to understand the concept of theodicy instantly and well enough to talk about it, so it seems the other dolls would be capable of eventually coming to the same conclusions.

Having an otherwise extraneous character mention the concept just inside earshot and having Leo act upon it the very next day is a little convenient for me (I'm surprised Leo came to philosophical grips with the concept so quickly as well). Were Leo's actions be spurred by gradual unease with his unnatural toy and then having DM contextualize theodicy in terms of the D&D campaign they were playing together would make it feel a touch less contrived to me.

That being said, that's a very minor quibble with an article I very much liked for multiple reasons right off the bat. Enthusiastic +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That sounds really cute tho.

+1

item.title: Author Posts are for Squares
item.content:encoded:
Well alright. New AR skip.

I got the idea for these little guys shortly after the 3000 Contest ended and I was wondering how to bring AR into Series IV. It wasn't until showing A Random Day the original draft that the article took its current tone and topics. Either way, I see your sad computer, GreenWolf, and raise you four sad, tiny, robots.

Special thanks to: Taffeta, minmin, SunnyClockwork, Roget, and Zyn for their help and feedback.

Extra special thanks to GreenWolf for use of his character Myra Ryder.

Super Extra Special thanks to A Random Day without whom this draft would not nearly be as polished or as punchy.

Enjoy the skip,

JC
